message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6181647/
**Title:** The possible role of brain rhythms in perceiving fast speech / theta-oscillator 'repackaging' (PMC)
**Found via:** acoustic-science/clear-speech search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract the key findings about how the brain perceives fast/time-compressed speech, the role of theta-band oscillations/brain rhythms, the "repackaging" hypothesis, and any quantitative limits on intelligible speech rate (syllables per second, compression factors). Report: (1) the article title, authors, and publish date; (2) specific falsifiable claims about theta oscillations, fast speech intelligibility limits, time-compression thresholds, and any mechanism for how silence insertion or repackaging helps. Include direct verbatim quotes for each claim.

content.content:
# Key Findings: Brain Rhythms and Fast Speech Perception

## Article Information
**Title:** "The possible role of brain rhythms in perceiving fast speech: Evidence from adult aging"
**Authors:** Lana R Penn, Nicole D Ayasse, Arthur Wingfield, Oded Ghitza
**Published:** October 11, 2018, *Journal of the Acoustical Society of America*, 144(4):2088–2094

---

## Key Falsifiable Claims

### 1. **Theta Oscillations and Speech Rate Correspondence**
The research proposes that syllables and words align with theta brain rhythms:
> "syllables, and words (mean duration of 250 ms) with theta (3–8 Hz) oscillations"

**Claim:** Theta oscillations (3–8 Hz) naturally synchronize with typical speech syllable rates, providing a neural "decoding window" for comprehension.

### 2. **Time-Compression Intelligibility Threshold**
Young adults show sharp intelligibility decline with extreme compression:
> "sharp decline in intelligibility for speech compressed by a factor of 3"

**Quantitative limit:** Speech compressed 3× (roughly doubling rate to ~9 syllables/second) causes marked intelligibility loss without intervention.

### 3. **Repackaging/Silence Insertion Rescues Comprehension**
The TEMPO model predicts that inserting silent gaps restores intelligibility:
> "intelligibility will remain high as long as theta is in sync with the input, but sharply decrease once the rhythm of the input exceeds the frequency range of theta"

When gaps are inserted at theta-compatible rates, "intelligibility was considerably restored."

### 4. **Age-Related Shift in Theta Frequency**
Older adults show earlier performance collapse and different rescue points:
> "the rescue point for the young adults occurred at 5 packets per second, at the center of the typical theta frequency range for young adults (3 to 8 Hz), while the rescue point for the older adults occurred at 4 packets per second"

**Mechanism claim:** Aging causes downward frequency shift in theta oscillations, creating mismatch with fast speech earlier than in younger listeners.

### 5. **Floor Effect in Older Adults**
> "older adults' accuracy was at floor level with speech rates as early as 12 syllables per second"

Young adults maintained higher accuracy at identical rates, supporting the oscillatory hypothesis's age-dependent predictions.

---

## Experimental Design Support
- **N=48** (24 young, 24 older adults; mean ages 21.6 and 74.5 years)
- **Speech rates tested:** 6–21 syllables/second
- **Repackaging rates:** 2–7 packets/second (theta range)
- **Stimuli:** Random digit strings (4 digits) to isolate bottom-up processing

content.text: Source successfully fetched. This is a peer-reviewed primary research article from the Journal of the Acoustical Society of America. Extracting falsifiable claims relevant to the research question.

claims.claim: Speech intelligibility depends on whether the input rhythm stays within the brain's theta-band frequency range (3-8 Hz); when input rate exceeds theta, intelligibility sharply decreases. This sets a neural ceiling on raw fast-speech comprehension that time-compression alone cannot overcome.
claims.quote: intelligibility will remain high as long as theta is in sync with the input, but sharply decrease once the rhythm of the input exceeds the frequency range of theta

claims.claim: Inserting silent gaps ('repackaging') into time-compressed speech at theta-compatible rates restores intelligibility, with the optimal restoration ('rescue point') at ~5 packets/second for young adults (center of the 3-8 Hz theta range). This means rhythmic restructuring, not just raw rate, governs comprehensibility at speed.
claims.quote: the rescue point for the young adults occurred at 5 packets per second, at the center of the typical theta frequency range for young adults (3 to 8 Hz), while the rescue point for the older adults occurred at 4 packets per second

claims.claim: Without intervention, intelligibility declines sharply when speech is time-compressed by a factor of 3 (roughly doubling natural rate), identifying a concrete compression threshold beyond which naive time-scaling fails.
claims.quote: sharp decline in intelligibility for speech compressed by a factor of 3

claims.claim: Older adults' accuracy hits floor level at speech rates as early as 12 syllables/second, while young adults sustain higher accuracy at the same rates, showing the intelligible-rate ceiling is listener-dependent (relevant to a 'trained power listener' vs general-student split).
claims.quote: older adults' accuracy was at floor level with speech rates as early as 12 syllables per second

claims.claim: Syllables and words (mean duration ~250 ms) correspond to theta oscillations (3-8 Hz), grounding the mechanism by which rhythm/pause structure of generated speech maps onto a neural decoding window.
claims.quote: syllables, and words (mean duration of 250 ms) with theta (3–8 Hz) oscillations

content.text: Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from this peer-reviewed primary research article (Penn, Ayasse, Wingfield & Ghitza, J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 2018). The source is highly relevant to research angle (1) acoustic/prosodic properties and pause/rhythm structure, and to angle (6) on measuring comprehensibility at speed. Its central contribution is the theta-oscillator "repackaging" finding: inserting silence to keep input rhythm within the 3-8 Hz theta band restores intelligibility of fast speech, which directly informs pause/rhythm design and the listener-dependent intelligible-rate ceiling.
